petitioner_background: The petitioner is a tile and stone business seeking to classify the beneficiary as an alien of extraordinary ability to work as a fabricator/installer for the business. The petition was self-represented. Initially, the petitioner submitted only a statement attesting to the beneficiary's job duties and skills. On appeal, the petitioner asserted that the beneficiary has reached a level whereby he can work unsupervised and offered to submit photographs of the petitioner's work, along with a blank pay stub.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to submit documentation relating to any of the ten regulatory criteria. The record did not establish that the beneficiary had distinguished himself as a fabricator/installer to such an extent that he could be said to have achieved sustained national or international acclaim or to be within the small percentage at the very top of his field. Even assuming the photographs offered on appeal might demonstrate that the beneficiary is a talented tile and stone fabricator/installer, the record did not reflect that he had attained any national acclaim for that talent. The burden of proof remained with the petitioner, who did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
